title: Sequential BIN Enumeration with Incremental Card Testing Pattern
narrative: Fraudster systematically tests sequential card numbers from a single BIN range (e.g., 453266XXXXXX1001 through 1050) using low-value authorization attempts. Within a 45-minute window, 30-80 unique card numbers from the same IIN appear, each attempting one or two transactions of $1.00 to $4.99 at the same merchant or MID. The pattern shows strict sequential ordering in card1 values with increments of 1-5 between attempts. All transactions share identical P_emaildomain (often disposable services like tempmail or guerrillamail), same addr1, and identical dist1 values indicating single origination point. Transactions cluster in is_night=1 periods (txn_hour between 1-5 AM merchant local time) to avoid real-time fraud monitoring. The card1_txn_count_24h metric shows 0 for all cards, indicating fresh enumeration. Failed transactions show AVS mismatch patterns while CVV may be guessed (often 999, 123, or sequential patterns).
indicators: 30-80 unique card1 values from identical 6-digit BIN within 45-minute window
Card numbers increment sequentially with gaps of 1-5 between consecutive attempts
TransactionAmt concentrated between $1.00-$4.99 for authorization testing
100% of attempts share identical P_emaildomain from disposable email services
All transactions show card1_txn_count_24h = 0 indicating untested card numbers
Identical addr1 and dist1 values across all sequential card attempts
Transaction timing clusters in txn_hour 1-5 AM with is_night=1
summary: Within 45 minutes, 30-80 untested card numbers from the same bank appear in sequential order (incrementing by 1-5), each making a single low-value transaction ($1-$5) using identical email addresses from disposable services and the same billing address, typically occurring between 1-5 AM.
reasoning: Criminals are systematically guessing valid card numbers within a stolen BIN range to identify active accounts for later high-value fraud, testing at night with small amounts to avoid detection while building a list of confirmed working cards.
indicator_explanations: Attacker is systematically testing dozens of cards from the same bank
Card numbers follow a predictable counting pattern with small skips
Amounts are deliberately tiny to slip under fraud-detection radar
Every transaction uses the same throwaway email address
None of these cards have been used anywhere in the past day
Billing address and distance fields are cloned across all attempts
Attack unfolds in the dead of night when monitoring is weakest
